original_abstract:
Traffic congestion remains a critical urban challenge, contributing to significant fuel waste, economic productivity loss, and delays for emergency services. Traditional traffic management relies on Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) using rigid round-robin scheduling, which fails to adapt to real-time fluctuations. While recent IoT-based solutions have attempted to use traffic density for control, they often struggle with accuracy—particularly regarding emergency vehicle detection—due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality image datasets for training deep neural networks. 

This paper introduces a novel, multi-modal distributed IoT framework designed around two key practical observations. First, recognizing that road infrastructure changes infrequently, we propose a two-stage vehicle detector ("R-YOLO") that uses road masking to focus computational resources. This approach achieves 79.5% detection accuracy on the UA-DETRAC dataset while maintaining the low latency required for edge deployment. Second, to address the limitations of visual detection for priority vehicles, we implement an acoustic detection algorithm on an edge device to identify the unique siren signatures of emergency vehicles. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system detects emergency vehicles with an average accuracy of 99.4%, providing a high-precision, resilient solution for intelligent urban traffic management.
humanized_abstract:
Traffic congestion is still one of the problems that have not yet been resolved in the urban area. Traffic congestion causes a lot of fuel consumption, loss of productivity and also cause delay to ambulances and fire trucks. Current traffic management systems that use Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) using a fixed round-robin scheduling algorithm that does not take into account the dynamic condition of traffic in the field. Newer smart traffic management systems that use IoT (Internet of Things) technology aim to regulate traffic movement based on traffic density. However, there are many problems that arise in this system, such as the inaccuracy of detecting emergency vehicles. This is because the images used to train the deep learning-based models in the detection system are limited and lack diversity and quality.

In this work, we present a multi-modal distributed IoT system for smart roads, taking into account two practical considerations: 1) that road infrastructure changes rarely and therefore that the computation resources on the edge devices can be utilized efficiently using a two-stage vehicle detector named “R-YOLO” that utilizes road masking and achieves 79.5% detection accuracy on the UA-DETRAC dataset while still meeting the latency requirements of an edge device; 2) that visual detection for priority vehicles is not always possible and therefore acoustic detection for emergency vehicles can be implemented on the edge device and our experiments show that it achieves an average accuracy of 99.4%. Therefore, our system achieves high accuracy and reliability.

document.input:
Traffic congestion remains a critical urban challenge, contributing to significant fuel waste, economic productivity loss, and delays for emergency services.
